target.name: (+)-denobilone A
target.motivation: Spirocyclic scaffolds are ubiquitous in natural products, pharmaceuticals, bioactive molecules, catalysts, ligands and advanced materials, particularly occupying a privileged position in pharmaceuticals with profound significance due to their unique three-dimensional architectures and structural novelty. (+)-denobilone A is a naturally occurring spirocyclic ortho-cyclohexadienone isolated from the stems of Dendrobium nobile with cytotoxic activity on cancer cell.

success_criteria.free_text: Enantioselective total synthesis of (+)-denobilone A with high enantioselectivity.

key_disconnections.reasoning: The key spirocyclic ortho-cyclohexadienone core of (+)-denobilone A can be formed through a dearomative spirocyclization of a biaryl precursor, which is a powerful strategy for constructing three-dimensional spirocycles from planar arenes.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy involves a photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization of an N-hydroxyphthalimide ester precursor. This approach leverages the stereoselectivity of engineered enzymes with visible-light activation to achieve enantioselective formation of the challenging chiral quaternary spirocenter. The precursor for (+)-denobilone A is derived from a known intermediate, which is then functionalized with an NHPI ester for the radical cyclization.

thought.background: Biocatalytic dearomatization for spirocycle formation is underexplored, with existing methods predominantly confined to heme-dependent enzymes. Photobiocatalysis, especially with visible-light-excited enzymes, has shown promise for asymmetric synthesis under mild conditions, but stereoselective synthesis of challenging spirocyclic compounds with congested chiral spiro centers remains unexplored.
thought.gap: There is a need for a biocatalytic platform that can achieve enantioselective dearomative spirocyclization, particularly for complex spirocyclic architectures.
thought.decision: To address this, we will develop a photoenzymatic platform using a nicotinamide-dependent ketoreductase (KRED) as a non-heme photobiocatalyst for stereoselective dearomatization to synthesize chiral spirocycles.

outcome.observation: Initial hypothesis formed to explore photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization using KRED.

evidence.text_span: In this work, inspired by the powerful strategy of dearomative spirocyclization and photobiocatalysis, we develop a photoenzymatic platform with nicotinamide-dependent ketoreductase (KRED) as a non-heme photobiocatalyst to realize the stereoselective dearomatization for the synthesis of chiral spirocycles (Fig. 1c).

thought.background: The hypothesis is to use a photoenzymatic approach for dearomative spirocyclization.
thought.gap: Need to identify suitable enzymes and initial reaction conditions for the target transformation.
thought.decision: To establish the reaction, model substrate 1a with N-hydroxyphthalimide (NHPI) ester will be tested with various nicotinamide-dependent KREDs and flavin-dependent 'ene'-reductases (EREDs) under blue LED irradiation (440 nm) in DMSO/Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8) at 25 °C, expecting to observe dearomative spirocyclization.

outcome.observation: LKADH catalyzed the reaction with 9% yield and 98% ee.

evidence.text_span: In our initial studies, model substrate 1a with N-hydroxyphthalimide (NHPI) ester as a radical precursor46,49 was prepared, and tested in the dearomatization reaction catalyzed by nicotinamide-dependent KREDs or flavin-dependent “ene”-reductases (EREDs)51,66, which have been proven as visible-light activable enzymes with highly promiscuous. Under the irradiation by blue LEDs (440 nm), several enzymes were pleasingly found to catalyze the dearomative spirocyclization but in unsatisfactory yields (Table 1, entries 1–3 and Supplementary Table 1), among which KREDs variant P2-D12 provided the highest yield (33%) with excellent enantioselectivity (>99% ee value) for product 2a (Table 1, entry 2).
evidence.section: Establishment of the enantioselective photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction

thought.background: Initial screening showed LKADH gave 9% yield and 98% ee.
thought.gap: Need to find an enzyme with better catalytic efficiency.
thought.decision: To improve the yield, P2-D12 will be tested under the same conditions, expecting higher yield while maintaining enantioselectivity.

outcome.observation: P2-D12 provided the highest yield (33%) with excellent enantioselectivity (>99% ee).

evidence.text_span: In our initial studies, model substrate 1a with N-hydroxyphthalimide (NHPI) ester as a radical precursor46,49 was prepared, and tested in the dearomatization reaction catalyzed by nicotinamide-dependent KREDs or flavin-dependent “ene”-reductases (EREDs)51,66, which have been proven as visible-light activable enzymes with highly promiscuous. Under the irradiation by blue LEDs (440 nm), several enzymes were pleasingly found to catalyze the dearomative spirocyclization but in unsatisfactory yields (Table 1, entries 1–3 and Supplementary Table 1), among which KREDs variant P2-D12 provided the highest yield (33%) with excellent enantioselectivity (>99% ee value) for product 2a (Table 1, entry 2).
evidence.section: Establishment of the enantioselective photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction

thought.background: P2-D12 showed the best initial performance (33% yield, >99% ee).
thought.gap: The yield is still unsatisfactory. Need to optimize reaction parameters.
thought.decision: To improve the yield, the light source will be optimized. Blue light (410 nm) will be tested, expecting an improved yield.

outcome.observation: Blue light (410 nm) provided product 2a with improved yield (37%) with comparable enantioselectivity (>99% ee).

evidence.text_span: With the optimization of the light source, blue light (410 nm) provided product 2a with improved yield (37%) with comparable enantioselectivity (>99% ee value), and higher λmax of light source than 440 nm would diminish the yield or enantioselectivity (Table 1, entries 4–5 and Supplementary Table 2).
evidence.section: Establishment of the enantioselective photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction

thought.background: Optimization of light source to 410 nm improved yield to 37%.
thought.gap: Need to further improve the yield. Other reaction parameters should be optimized.
thought.decision: To further optimize, the cosolvent will be changed from DMSO to DMF, expecting better efficiency.

outcome.observation: Replacement of DMSO by DMF exhibited worse efficiency for this reaction, yielding 26%.

evidence.text_span: Further decreasing the enzyme loading (Supplementary Table 3) and replacement of the cosolvent DMSO by other aprotic or protic solvents (Table 1, entries 6–7 and Supplementary Table 4) exhibited worse efficiency for this reaction.
evidence.section: Establishment of the enantioselective photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction

thought.background: DMF as cosolvent resulted in lower yield (26%).
thought.gap: Need to find a more suitable cosolvent or revert to DMSO.
thought.decision: To further optimize, MeOH will be tested as a protic cosolvent, expecting better efficiency.

outcome.observation: Replacement of DMSO by MeOH exhibited worse efficiency for this reaction, yielding 5%.

evidence.text_span: Further decreasing the enzyme loading (Supplementary Table 3) and replacement of the cosolvent DMSO by other aprotic or protic solvents (Table 1, entries 6–7 and Supplementary Table 4) exhibited worse efficiency for this reaction.
evidence.section: Establishment of the enantioselective photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction

thought.background: Changing cosolvent to DMF or MeOH resulted in lower yields (26% and 5% respectively).
thought.gap: The cosolvent optimization was not successful. Need to optimize other parameters, specifically the buffer.
thought.decision: To improve the yield, buffer optimization will be performed. Citrate phosphate buffer at pH 8 will be tested, expecting better reaction performance.

outcome.observation: Citrate phosphate buffer at pH 8 improved the yield to 42%.

evidence.text_span: Buffer optimization revealed that buffer and its pH had a great influence on the reaction performance, and the employment of citrate phosphate (pH 5) could enhance the dearomative spirocyclization reaction yield to 56% (Table 1, entries 8–9 and Supplementary Table 5).
evidence.section: Establishment of the enantioselective photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction

thought.background: Citrate phosphate buffer at pH 8 improved the yield to 42%.
thought.gap: Need to further optimize the buffer pH.
thought.decision: To further improve the yield, citrate phosphate buffer at pH 5 will be tested, expecting a further enhancement in reaction yield.

outcome.observation: Citrate phosphate buffer at pH 5 enhanced the dearomative spirocyclization reaction yield to 56%.

evidence.text_span: Buffer optimization revealed that buffer and its pH had a great influence on the reaction performance, and the employment of citrate phosphate (pH 5) could enhance the dearomative spirocyclization reaction yield to 56% (Table 1, entries 8–9 and Supplementary Table 5).
evidence.section: Establishment of the enantioselective photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction

thought.background: Citrate phosphate buffer at pH 5 improved the yield to 56%.
thought.gap: Need to further optimize reaction parameters to maximize yield.
thought.decision: To further improve the yield, temperature will be optimized. The reaction will be run at 20 °C, expecting a higher yield without loss of enantioselectivity.

outcome.observation: Temperature optimization to 20 °C improved the yield to 79% without loss of enantioselectivity.

evidence.text_span: Temperature is also an important influencing factor for the photoenzymatic reaction, which finally improved the yield to 79% without the loss of enantioselectivity (Table 1, entry 10 and Supplementary Table 6).
evidence.section: Establishment of the enantioselective photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction

thought.background: Optimized conditions yielded 79% of 2a with >99% ee. Need to confirm the role of P2-D12.
thought.gap: Is the enzyme essential for the reaction?
thought.decision: To confirm the role of P2-D12, a control experiment will be performed without the enzyme, expecting no reactivity.

outcome.observation: No reactivity was observed in the control experiment lacking P2-D12 (<2% yield).

evidence.text_span: Moreover, no reactivity was observed in the control experiments lacking P2-D12 (Table 1, entry 11), blue LED (Table 1, entry 12), glucose dehydrogenase (GDH) (Table 1, entry 13) or oxidized form of nicotinamide-adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+ ) (Table 1, entry 14).
evidence.section: Establishment of the enantioselective photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction

thought.background: P2-D12 is essential. Need to confirm the role of blue LED.
thought.gap: Is blue LED irradiation necessary for the reaction?
thought.decision: To confirm the role of blue LED, a control experiment will be performed without blue LED, expecting no reactivity.

outcome.observation: No reactivity was observed in the control experiment lacking blue LED (<2% yield).

evidence.text_span: Moreover, no reactivity was observed in the control experiments lacking P2-D12 (Table 1, entry 11), blue LED (Table 1, entry 12), glucose dehydrogenase (GDH) (Table 1, entry 13) or oxidized form of nicotinamide-adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+ ) (Table 1, entry 14).
evidence.section: Establishment of the enantioselective photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction

thought.background: Blue LED is essential. Need to confirm the role of GDH.
thought.gap: Is GDH necessary for the reaction?
thought.decision: To confirm the role of GDH, a control experiment will be performed without GDH, expecting no reactivity.

outcome.observation: No reactivity was observed in the control experiment lacking GDH (<2% yield).

evidence.text_span: Moreover, no reactivity was observed in the control experiments lacking P2-D12 (Table 1, entry 11), blue LED (Table 1, entry 12), glucose dehydrogenase (GDH) (Table 1, entry 13) or oxidized form of nicotinamide-adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+ ) (Table 1, entry 14).
evidence.section: Establishment of the enantioselective photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction

thought.background: GDH is essential. Need to confirm the role of NADP+.
thought.gap: Is NADP+ necessary for the reaction?
thought.decision: To confirm the role of NADP+, a control experiment will be performed without NADP+, expecting no reactivity.

outcome.observation: No reactivity was observed in the control experiment lacking NADP+ (<2% yield).

evidence.text_span: Moreover, no reactivity was observed in the control experiments lacking P2-D12 (Table 1, entry 11), blue LED (Table 1, entry 12), glucose dehydrogenase (GDH) (Table 1, entry 13) or oxidized form of nicotinamide-adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+ ) (Table 1, entry 14).
evidence.section: Establishment of the enantioselective photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction

thought.background: The optimized conditions with P2-D12 yielded 79% of 2a with >99% ee. However, further improvement in yield is desired.
thought.gap: The active site of P2-D12 might be too small or have unfavorable interactions, limiting catalytic efficiency.
thought.decision: To further improve the yield, a structure-guided semi-rational mutagenesis strategy will be utilized to engineer P2-D12, expecting to enhance the catalytic efficiency by modifying the enzyme's active site.

outcome.observation: Decision to use semi-rational engineering of P2-D12.

evidence.text_span: To further improve the yield of desired product 2a, a structure-guided semi-rational mutagenesis strategy was utilized.
evidence.section: Semi-rational engineering of P2-D12 for efficient synthesis of 2a

thought.background: Molecular docking of substrate 1a into P2-D12 revealed potential steric hindrance and a relatively small active pocket, which might limit the reaction efficiency. Specifically, L195 was identified as a residue interacting with the substrate and cofactor NADPH.
thought.gap: The steric hindrance and small active site volume might be limiting the yield.
thought.decision: To improve the yield, alanine-scanning mutagenesis of surrounding amino acids will be performed. Specifically, L195 will be mutated to alanine (L195A), expecting to increase the active site volume and facilitate electron transfer.

outcome.observation: The mutation of L195 to alanine (P2-D12-L195A) manifested the highest yield of 2a (91%) with excellent ee value (>99%). Structural analysis showed an increased active site volume and shorter EDA complex distance.

evidence.text_span: Docking results showed that interactions included strong hydrogen bonding interactions between the substrate and Q153, carbon hydrogen bonding and π-anion interactions between the substrate and D150, π- alkyl interactions between the benzene rings of 1a and A94, P151, L195 and M205, as well as alkyl-alkyl interactions at methyl on the substrate with M205 and M206 (Fig. 2b). In addition, relatively small active pocket and the significant steric hindrance between substrate and cofactor NADPH were observed (Fig. 2a), which might result in the poor result of this reaction. After alanine-scanning of the surrounding amino acids, the mutation of residue L195 between substrate and cofactor NADPH to alanine manifested the highest yield of 2a (91%) still with excellent ee value (>99%) (Fig. 2c and Supplementary Table 7).
evidence.section: Semi-rational engineering of P2-D12 for efficient synthesis of 2a

thought.background: The photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction with P2-D12-L195A achieved 91% yield and >99% ee for model substrate 1a. This optimized method shows promise for practical applications.
thought.gap: Need to demonstrate the synthetic utility of this reaction on a preparative scale.
thought.decision: To investigate synthetic utility, a preparative scale reaction will be conducted on a 0.5 mmol scale, expecting to produce a significant amount of spirocyclic para-cyclohexadienone 2a with good isolated yield and enantioselectivity.

outcome.observation: Preparative scale synthesis on 0.5 mmol scale produced 70 mg of spirocyclic para-cyclohexadienone 2a in 69% isolated yield with 92% ee.

evidence.text_span: To investigate the synthetic utility of this photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization reaction, preparative scale reaction was conducted on a 0.5 mmol scale, producing spirocyclic para-cyclohexadienone 2a (70 mg) in 69% isolated yield with 92% ee value (Fig. 4a).

thought.background: The optimized photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization is effective for model substrate 1a. The method has potential for natural product synthesis.
thought.gap: Need to apply this methodology to the enantioselective total synthesis of a complex natural product, specifically (+)-denobilone A, which has a challenging spirocyclic ortho-cyclohexadienone core.
thought.decision: To demonstrate the utility, the method will be applied to the enantioselective total synthesis of (+)-denobilone A. Key intermediate 3a will be synthesized, followed by NHPI ester installation to produce 3b, which will then undergo photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization using engineered P2-D12 variants.

outcome.observation: Key intermediate 3a was synthesized according to literature, and subsequently the NHPI ester was installed to produce 3b.

evidence.text_span: Encouraged by the result, we applied the method to the enantioselective total synthesis of (+)-denobilone A, a naturally occurring spirocyclic orthocyclohexadienone isolated from the stems of Dendrobium nobile with cytotoxic activity on cancer cell70, although previous synthetic efforts have already yielded the racemic form69. We synthesized the key intermediate 3a according to the literature, and subsequently installed the NHPI ester to produce 3b, which served as the substrate of the established photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization.

thought.background: Substrate 3b for (+)-denobilone A synthesis has been prepared. The photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization needs to be applied.
thought.gap: The optimal enzyme variant for this specific substrate (3b) needs to be identified, as the substrate scope might require different enzyme engineering.
thought.decision: To achieve enantioselective synthesis of (+)-denobilone A, substrate 3b will be subjected to photoenzymatic dearomative spirocyclization using various engineered P2-D12 mutants (e.g., P2-D12-Q153Y/M205F) under blue LED irradiation (410 nm), expecting to obtain (+)-denobilone A with high ee.

outcome.observation: (+)-denobilone A (3c) was obtained with 93% ee, although with a suboptimal yield (10%) compared to the racemic photocatalytic reaction (33% yield).

evidence.text_span: After screening several mutants (Supplementary Table 9 and Supplementary Fig. 2), (+)-denobilone A (3c) could be obtained with 93% ee value delightfully (Fig. 4b), the stereochemistry of which was confirmed by its specific rotation based on the previous characterization data70. Despite the suboptimal yield compared with that of the racemic photocatalytic reaction (33% yield), this biocatalytic route represents a significant advancement towards the sustainable and enantioselective synthesis of denobilone A, providing a valuable pattern for the construction of pharmaceutically relevant molecules.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Yield for the total synthesis of (+)-denobilone A.

validation.comparison_to_prior: The biocatalytic route provides enantioselective synthesis of (+)-denobilone A (93% ee), which was previously synthesized in racemic form (33% yield for the photocatalytic step). While the yield for the biocatalytic step is lower (10%), it represents a significant advancement in enantioselectivity and sustainability.
validation.significance: This work establishes a photobiocatalytic platform for the enantioselective synthesis of spirocyclic compounds, addressing a significant void in sustainable synthetic methodologies. It repurposes and rationally engineers a nicotinamide-dependent ketoreductase (P2-D12-L195A) as a visible-light-excited photobiocatalyst, overcoming challenges in stereocontrol at congested all-carbon quaternary spirocenters. The methodology demonstrates broad substrate scope, excellent enantioselectivities, and moderate to excellent yields under mild and aqueous conditions, with practical synthetic utility underscored by the enantioselective total synthesis of (+)-denobilone A. This expands the biocatalytic toolbox for complex chiral architectures and suggests broader applicability of visible-light-excited enzymes for radical transformations.

source.title: Photoenzymatic catalysis enables enantioselective radical dearomative spirocyclization.
source.authors: Zhu C, Zhang Y, Deng Z, Ji L, Chen X, Yin D, Liu C, Luo Z, Rao Y, Yuan Z.
